Hypertension, diagnosed 4 years ago, well-controlled on medication
Osteoporosis, diagnosed 3 years ago, on calcium and vitamin D supplementation
Chronic lower back pain with intermittent radiculopathy
Remote history of treated Helicobacter pylori gastritis, 5 years ago

medications.frequency: as needed, up to three times daily

family_history: Mother died at age 82 of dementia (type not specified)
Father died at age 78 of stomach cancer
One sibling (brother, age 71) with mild cognitive impairment
No family history of stroke or movement disorders

social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinker, less than once monthly
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in apartment; daughter visits twice weekly
social_history.exercise: Previously walked 30 minutes daily; stopped 2 months ago due to unsteadiness
social_history.diet: Regular Korean diet, no specific restrictions

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and year but not the exact date (states month incorrectly). MMSE score 23/30 (lost points on orientation 4/10, recall 0/3, serial 7s 3/5). Able to name common objects but had word-finding pauses. Unable to recall 3 words after 5 minutes even with categorical cues. Clock drawing mildly abnormal with misplaced numbers on right side.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive to light. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation and strength symmetric. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. No pronator drift. Muscle bulk and tone normal throughout. No rigidity or cogwheeling noted.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Proprioception intact at bilateral great toes. Mild decreased vibration sense at bilateral ankles, consistent with age.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric in bilateral upper extremities. 2+ at bilateral knees. 1+ at bilateral ankles. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Hoffmann sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing intact. No intention tremor.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait with shortened stride length, mildly wide-based. Difficulty with tandem walking. Turns require multiple steps. Romberg negative. No festination. Gait initiation intact without freezing.
neurological_exam.additional: Frontal release signs: glabellar reflex present (does not habituate after 5 taps), palmomental reflex absent bilaterally. No grasp reflex. Paratonia noted on passive arm movements bilaterally.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive memory problems and gait difficulty over the past several months
patient.history_present_illness: This 74-year-old right-handed woman, a retired elementary school teacher, presents to the neurology outpatient clinic with her daughter for evaluation of progressive cognitive decline and gait disturbance over approximately five months. The daughter first noticed subtle changes around five months ago when her mother began repeating the same questions within a single conversation and forgetting recent phone calls. Over the ensuing weeks, the patient reportedly missed several scheduled medical appointments, which was highly uncharacteristic of her previously meticulous organizational habits. Approximately three months ago the patient began having difficulty managing her household finances, resulting in several unpaid utility bills that the daughter discovered incidentally. The patient herself acknowledges some forgetfulness but minimizes the severity, attributing it to normal aging and poor sleep. Her daughter reports that the patient has become more socially withdrawn, declining invitations from longtime friends and spending more time at home alone. Approximately two months ago, the patient developed gait unsteadiness with several near-falls while walking in her apartment, prompting her to stop her daily walking routine. She has not had any witnessed falls with loss of consciousness or head trauma. She denies any episodes of urinary incontinence, visual hallucinations, or fluctuating levels of alertness. There have been no personality or behavioral changes, no compulsive behaviors, and no disinhibition noted by her daughter. The patient reports occasional word-finding difficulty in conversation, particularly when trying to recall names of acquaintances, though she remains conversational and coherent. She denies headaches, fever, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. Sleep has been fragmented with frequent awakenings, but there is no history of dream-enactment behavior or restless legs. The patient's primary care physician checked basic labs three months ago and was told everything was normal, though her daughter is uncertain of the specific tests performed. No prior neuroimaging or cognitive testing has been done.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8 Hz alpha rhythm, borderline low amplitude, reactive to eye opening
background.state_changes: Patient drowsy during portions of recording with increased theta activity

artifacts.impact: Intermittent obscuring of temporal activity during periods of jaw clenching

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient age and cooperation

eeg.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG for age. Borderline posterior dominant rhythm at 8 Hz with intermittent generalized theta slowing and focal left temporal slowing. No epileptiform discharges identified. Findings are nonspecific and may be seen in early neurodegenerative disease, metabolic encephalopathy, or medication effects. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Patient drowsy during portions of the study, which limits interpretation of mild background slowing. Intermittent temporal muscle artifact partially obscures temporal channels.

findings.location: Bilateral medial temporal lobes and hippocampi
findings.size: Scheltens medial temporal atrophy score: Grade 2 bilaterally

signal_characteristics.T1: Reduced hippocampal volume with widening of choroidal fissures and temporal horns

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No periventricular signal abnormality to suggest transependymal CSF flow

findings.location: Periventricular and deep white matter, bilateral

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Hyperintense lesions in periventricular regions and scattered in deep white matter of frontal and parietal lobes

signal_characteristics.T1: Widened sulci in bilateral temporal and parietal regions

volumetrics.hippocampal_volume: Left 2.8 mL, Right 2.9 mL (below 15th percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: No abnormal enhancement on post-contrast sequences
No mass lesion or midline shift
Minimal mucosal thickening in bilateral maxillary sinuses (incidental)
Flow voids preserved in major intracranial vessels
mri.impression: Mild bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens grade 2) with subtle temporoparietal cortical volume loss, which may be seen in early neurodegenerative processes including Alzheimer's disease but can also represent age-related changes. No acute intracranial pathology. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Mild left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (SV1 + RV5 = 36 mm)
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 74 bpm. Mild left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria, likely related to longstanding hypertension. No acute ischemic changes. No arrhythmia.

abnormal_values_summary: Fasting glucose 108 mg/dL (mildly elevated, prediabetic range)

Neuropsychological Testing Summary.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate
Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: MMSE 23/30 score (H), MoCA 19/30 score (H), Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate 28 words (5 trials) (H), Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed 2 words (H), Boston Naming Test 48/60 score (H), Clock Drawing Test 3/5 score (H), Geriatric Depression Scale 6/15 score (H).

additional_observations: White matter uptake within normal limits
No focal cortical abnormalities on CT component
output.impression: Positive amyloid PET scan with moderate to high cortical amyloid deposition, most prominent in posterior cingulate/precuneus and temporoparietal regions. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.location: Bilateral temporoparietal cortices and posterior cingulate/precuneus

signal_characteristics.pattern: Bilateral temporoparietal and posterior cingulate hypometabolism

output.impression: FDG-PET demonstrates bilateral temporoparietal and posterior cingulate/precuneus hypometabolism with relative preservation of frontal, occipital, and primary sensorimotor cortex metabolism. Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.NfL: 1680 pg/mL (mildly elevated; reference <1500 pg/mL for age)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 12 cm. WBC: 2 cells/mm3 (normal) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 38 mg/dL. Glucose: 62 mg/dL (ratio 0.58 (serum glucose 107 mg/dL; normal ratio)). Abeta42: 412 pg/mL (low; reference >500 pg/mL). Abeta42_40_ratio: 0.062 (low; reference >0.089). phospho_tau_181: 68 pg/mL (elevated; reference <60 pg/mL). total_tau: 486 pg/mL (elevated; reference <400 pg/mL). NfL: 1680 pg/mL (mildly elevated; reference <1500 pg/mL for age). cryptococcal_antigen: Negative. fungal_culture: No growth at 4 weeks. bacterial_culture: No growth. AFB_stain: Negative. TB_PCR: Negative. cytology: No malignant cells.

abnormal_values_summary: APOE e3/e4 genotype (heterozygous e4 carrier, increased AD risk)

output.query: normal pressure hydrocephalus versus Alzheimer's disease differential diagnosis ventriculomegaly

results.title: Differentiating idiopathic normal pressure hydrocephalus from Alzheimer's disease: a systematic review
results.key_finding: The classic NPH triad (gait disturbance, dementia, urinary incontinence) overlaps significantly with AD. Key differentiators include: disproportionate ventriculomegaly relative to sulcal enlargement (DESH sign) on MRI, gait improvement after large-volume lumbar puncture (tap test), and subcortical/frontal cognitive pattern in NPH versus amnestic pattern in AD.

results.title: Evans index and callosal angle in the diagnosis of normal pressure hydrocephalus
results.key_finding: Evans index >0.30 suggests ventriculomegaly. However, Evans index alone has limited specificity for NPH. Callosal angle <90 degrees is more specific for NPH. DESH (disproportionately enlarged subarachnoid-space hydrocephalus) pattern is the most reliable imaging criterion.

results.title: CSF biomarkers in NPH and AD: overlapping and distinguishing features
results.key_finding: NPH patients typically show normal or mildly decreased Abeta42 but normal tau levels, whereas AD patients show decreased Abeta42 with elevated tau and phospho-tau. Comorbid NPH and AD pathology occurs in approximately 25-30% of elderly patients with ventriculomegaly.

output.summary: NPH and AD share overlapping clinical features, particularly in elderly patients with cognitive decline and gait disturbance. Key distinctions: (1) MRI findings - NPH shows disproportionate ventriculomegaly with crowded vertex sulci (DESH pattern), while AD shows proportional ventriculomegaly with cortical atrophy; (2) Cognitive pattern - NPH produces subcortical/frontal deficits while AD produces amnestic deficits; (3) CSF biomarkers - AD shows decreased Abeta42 with elevated tau, while NPH shows normal tau; (4) Tap test - clinical improvement after 30-50 mL CSF removal supports NPH. Comorbid pathology is common.

output.proposed: Donepezil 5 mg once daily at bedtime
interactions: Donepezil + Pantoprazole: Minor interaction. Pantoprazole may slightly reduce donepezil absorption but is generally not clinically significant.
warnings: Donepezil may cause bradycardia; monitor heart rate given patient's resting HR of 72 bpm
GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea) are common; start at 5 mg and increase to 10 mg after 4-6 weeks if tolerated
May lower seizure threshold; monitor for any seizure activity
alternatives: Rivastigmine (oral or transdermal patch) - alternative cholinesterase inhibitor
Galantamine - alternative cholinesterase inhibitor with nicotinic modulation

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Gait assessment (pre-tap) - 10m walk time 18.2 seconds (H), Gait assessment (post-tap 2hr) - 10m walk time 17.5 seconds (H), Gait assessment (post-tap 24hr) - 10m walk time 17.8 seconds (H), MMSE (pre-tap) 23/30 score (H), MMSE (post-tap 24hr) 23/30 score (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Gait velocity remains impaired pre- and post-tap
No significant improvement in gait or cognition after CSF drainage

differential.key_distinguishing: Ventriculomegaly on MRI and gait disturbance raise concern, but Evans index is borderline, no DESH pattern, normal opening pressure, negative tap test, and amnestic cognitive pattern are inconsistent with NPH
Fazekas grade 2 white matter disease and prediabetic vascular risk factors suggest possible vascular contribution, but predominant amnestic deficit and biomarker profile favor AD as primary etiology

Mildly elevated GDS score (6/15) but below threshold for major depression; severity of memory deficit on neuropsych testing disproportionate to mood symptoms; true organic amnesia (encoding failure) not explained by depression alone
Normal CSF cell count and protein, negative cryptococcal antigen, negative TB PCR, negative cultures effectively exclude chronic meningitis
No visual hallucinations, no fluctuating cognition, no parkinsonism (no rigidity or cogwheeling), no REM sleep behavior disorder

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI to evaluate structural causes of cognitive decline and gait disturbance
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens grade 2), mild ventriculomegaly, Fazekas grade 2 white matter disease

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup including dementia screening panel (TSH, B12, folate, RPR, HIV, CBC, BMP)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal dementia screening; incidental prediabetic findings (fasting glucose 108, HbA1c 6.1%)

optimal_actions.action: Request formal neuropsychological testing to characterize cognitive deficit pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Amnestic pattern with severely impaired delayed recall, consistent with hippocampal dysfunction

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to assess for subclinical seizures or other electrical abnormalities
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Borderline PDR at 8 Hz, mild diffuse slowing, no epileptiform discharges

optimal_actions.action: Order amyloid PET or CSF biomarkers to confirm AD pathology given moderate diagnostic uncertainty
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive amyloid PET with temporoparietal and posterior cingulate uptake

optimal_actions.action: Consider NPH evaluation with tap test given ventriculomegaly and gait disturbance
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Negative tap test with no gait or cognitive improvement

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions before initiating cholinesterase inhibitor therapy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No major contraindications to donepezil; monitor for bradycardia

optimal_actions.action: Search literature on NPH versus AD differential to inform clinical reasoning
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Key differentiators between NPH and AD including imaging, cognitive pattern, and CSF biomarkers

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI to evaluate for structural causes of cognitive decline and gait disturbance
Order dementia screening labs (TSH, B12, RPR) to exclude reversible causes
Perform neuropsychological testing to characterize the cognitive deficit pattern
Confirm AD pathology with biomarkers (amyloid PET or CSF Abeta42/tau) given moderate diagnostic uncertainty
Differentiate from NPH given ventriculomegaly and gait symptoms
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing NPH and proceeding to ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement without adequate workup (negative tap test, amnestic cognitive pattern inconsistent with NPH)
Attributing cognitive decline solely to depression without objective cognitive testing
Starting anticholinergic medications which would worsen cognitive function
Prescribing benzodiazepines for anxiety or sleep without considering cognitive impact
Performing invasive intracranial procedures without confirming diagnosis
key_reasoning_points: The combination of progressive amnestic cognitive decline and gait disturbance in an elderly woman initially suggests a broad differential including NPH, AD, vascular dementia, and chronic meningitis
MRI shows bilateral hippocampal atrophy and mild ventriculomegaly, creating diagnostic ambiguity between AD with ex vacuo changes and NPH

The amnestic pattern on neuropsychological testing (severely impaired delayed recall with relatively preserved executive function) is characteristic of AD rather than the subcortical/frontal pattern seen in NPH
CSF biomarkers (decreased Abeta42, elevated phospho-tau and total tau) confirm AD pathology and argue against NPH as the primary diagnosis
Negative tap test provides further evidence against NPH as the primary etiology

The source case involved cryptococcal meningitis mimicking NPH; in this reimagined case, the key diagnostic challenge is distinguishing AD from NPH, with white matter disease adding vascular cognitive impairment as a contributing factor
Prediabetic vascular risk factors (elevated fasting glucose, HbA1c 6.1%) and Fazekas grade 2 white matter disease suggest possible mixed AD-vascular pathology
Family history of dementia in mother and MCI in sibling, combined with APOE e3/e4 genotype, increases the prior probability of AD

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty due to overlapping features with NPH (ventriculomegaly, gait disturbance) and vascular cognitive impairment (white matter disease, prediabetic risk factors). The amnestic cognitive pattern and biomarker profile ultimately favor AD, but the diagnostic path requires systematic exclusion of NPH and reversible causes. Incidental prediabetic findings add a red herring vascular component.
